aids.title: C-E-N: Polynomial Node Fields
aids.content: Coefficient, Exponent, Next pointer. C-E-N -- every polynomial node has exactly these three fields. In the struct: int coeff, int expo, struct PolyNode *next.
aids.forRemembering: The structure of a polynomial linked list node

aids.title: Merge Sort Analogy for Addition
aids.content: Polynomial addition works like merging two sorted arrays. Both lists are sorted by exponent (descending). Walk both simultaneously, compare exponents, and merge into the result -- just like the merge step in merge sort. Time complexity: O(n1 + n2), same as merge.
aids.forRemembering: The polynomial addition algorithm using linked lists

aids.title: EGL: Three Cases in Addition
aids.content: Equal exponents → Add coefficients (skip if sum is zero!). Greater exponent → Copy that term to result. Lesser exponent → Move to next term. EGL like 'eagle' soaring over the two lists.
aids.forRemembering: The three comparison cases when adding polynomials

aids.title: Multiply: Coeff×Coeff, Expo+Expo
aids.content: For multiplication, remember: Coefficients MULTIPLY (product), Exponents ADD (sum). Example: 3x² × 2x³ = 6x⁵. Then use insertTerm() to combine like terms automatically. Complexity: O(n1 × n2 × n).
aids.forRemembering: How polynomial multiplication computes new terms

aids.title: Sparse = Parking Lot Analogy
aids.content: An array for a sparse polynomial is like reserving a 1000-car parking lot for 3 cars. Linked list is like valet parking -- you only use spots for cars that actually exist. For 3x¹⁰⁰⁰ + 2x + 7: Array = 1001 slots (O(d)), Linked List = 3 nodes (O(n)).
aids.forRemembering: Why linked lists are preferred over arrays for sparse polynomials

aids.title: DAZE: Decreasing And Zero Elimination
aids.content: DAZE -- two golden rules of polynomial linked lists: (1) Decreasing order of exponents always maintained, (2) Zero coefficient terms are Always Eliminated (never stored). Examiners love testing both these details.
aids.forRemembering: Two critical rules for polynomial linked list representation
